coredata.dc:title: Enhancing visible-light-driven photocatalytic degradation of nitric oxide with lignite-derived graphene quantum dots/BiOBr heterojunctions
coredata.dc:creator: Nie, Qianqian
Jia, Liuhu
Cui, Yunpei
Luan, Jianfu
Tan, Zhongchao
Yu, Hesheng
coredata.dc:description: In this work, lignite was used to prepare value-added graphene quantum dots (L-GQDs) via the chemical oxidation process. The prepared L-GQDs were then hybridized with bismuth oxybromide (BiOBr) to form composite photocatalysts by a semi-solvothermal method. The composites catalytically degraded NO under visible-light irradiation. Multiple characterizations demonstrated that L-GQDs were successfully deposited onto BiOBr without changing the valence states of the elements in BiOBr. The addition of L-GQDs not only boosted the NO removal efficiency from 48.44% to 80.17%, but also significantly improved the environmental friendliness of the catalyst. Experimental investigations and theoretical calculations confirmed the formation of a type II heterojunction between L-GQDs and BiOBr. Such a heterojunction facilitated the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes, thus enhancing the photocatalytic activity. Furthermore, the prepared composite catalyst demonstrated good stability, maintaining 94.40% of its original photocatalytic activity after five cycles. The synthesis and environmentally conscious application of the value-added L-GQDs in this work provides a cleaner alternative for utilizing low-rank coals.

head.ce:title: Enhancing visible-light-driven photocatalytic degradation of nitric oxide with lignite-derived graphene quantum dots/BiOBr heterojunctions

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, lignite was used to prepare value-added graphene quantum dots (L-GQDs) via the chemical oxidation process. The prepared L-GQDs were then hybridized with bismuth oxybromide (BiOBr) to form composite photocatalysts by a semi-solvothermal method. The composites catalytically degraded NO under visible-light irradiation. Multiple characterizations demonstrated that L-GQDs were successfully deposited onto BiOBr without changing the valence states of the elements in BiOBr. The addition of L-GQDs not only boosted the NO removal efficiency from 48.44% to 80.17%, but also significantly improved the environmental friendliness of the catalyst. Experimental investigations and theoretical calculations confirmed the formation of a type II heterojunction between L-GQDs and BiOBr. Such a heterojunction facilitated the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes, thus enhancing the photocatalytic activity. Furthermore, the prepared composite catalyst demonstrated good stability, maintaining 94.40% of its original photocatalytic activity after five cycles. The synthesis and environmentally conscious application of the value-added L-GQDs in this work provides a cleaner alternative for utilizing low-rank coals.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The burning of fossil fuels, particularly low rank coal such as lignite, significantly contributes to the release of nitrogen oxides (NOx) into the atmosphere. These emissions have severe consequences, including photochemical smog and adverse impacts on human health [1,2]. Lignite, characterized by its preserved macromolecular structures from ancient plants material, remains a dominant fuel for power generation [3–5]. However, due to its inherent properties, lignite combustion suffers from low energy conversion efficiency and substantial NOx generation. To address these challenges, researchers are exploring alternative approaches. Increasingly more efforts are directed towards utilizing lignite as a material rather than fuel. We can then reduce environmental pollution associated with its combustion by utilizing lignite in innovation ways. This shift in the way of utilizing lignite aims to enhance both energy efficiency and environmental sustainability.

Current methods to control NOx emissions from lignite combustion mainly include selective catalytic reduction, adsorption, and wet scrubbing [6–8]. However, these approaches face challenges such as high investment cost, high operation costs, and restricted efficiency. As a result, there is a pressing need for a more cost-effective NOx control solution. Recently, photocatalysis has emerged as a promising alternative. Its advantages lie in its simplicity operation, mild reaction conditions, and low energy consumption [9]. Given that nitric oxide (NO) constitutes 90–95 % of NOx in typical flue gas streams, various photocatalysts have been designed specifically for NO photodegradation [10]. By harnessing the power of light-driven reactions, photocatalysis offers a pathway to mitigate NOx emissions from lignite combustion effectively.

One promising way to maximize the potential of lignite while minimizing environmental impact is to convert lignite into carbon nanomaterials. Carbon nanotubes, graphene oxide, carbon dots, and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) can all be synthesized from lignite [11–13]. Among them, GQDs stand out as one of the most promising carbon nanomaterials due to their unique properties. GQDs comprise one or several layers of graphene sheets with lateral dimensions less than 10 nm, and possesses a band gap. They demonstrate strong quantum-limited and edge effects, rendering them fluorescent [14–16]. Additionally, GQDs have low cytotoxicity and hold significant research values across diverse fields, including biomedicine, photovoltaic conversion, and catalytic processes [17,18].

In the area of photocatalysis, bismuth oxybromide (BiOBr) has received widespread attention. Its suitable band gap, low preparation cost, and stable physicochemical properties make is an attractive candidate [19–21]. However, BiOBr faces limitations, including ineffective visible light adsorption, poor separation of photogenerated electron-hole pair, and a small specific surface area, all of which hinder its photocatalytic activity [22]. To address those challenges, researchers have explored heterojunctions [23]. By combining BiOBr with various semiconductors such as Bi2O3, BiPO4, FeWO4, and BiOI, charge separation can be improved, ultimately enhancing photocatalytic performance [24–27].

GQDs have gained attention for their unique properties in various applications. Their composite with BiOBr to enhance photocatalytic activity remains unexplored, albeit that they have been hybridized with certain photocatalysts such as g-C3N4 [28] and Bi2WO6 [29]. In this context, we proposed to apply heterojunctions formed between lignite-derived GQDs (L-GQDs) and BiOBr to achieve photocatalytic degradation of NOx. This innovative approach not only transforms lignite into value-added L-GQDs, but also offers a promising solution for mitigating NOx emissions from lignite combustion in an environmentally responsible manner.

In the present study, lignite is converted into L-GQDs via chemical oxidation. These L-GQDs are then employed to synthesize L-GQDs/BiOBr composites using a semi-solvothermal method. The prepared composite catalysts are then extensively characterized by various instruments. Subsequently, the photocatalytic activity of the prepared catalysts is evaluated through the photocatalytic degradation of NO. Finally, the underlying mechanism is comprehensively explored through a combination of experimental investigations, instrumental analyses, and density functional theory (DFT) calculations.

The reagents used in this study include bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3⋅5H2O), potassium bromide (KBr), sulfuric acid (H2SO4), nitric acid (HNO3), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), potassium iodide (KI), anhydrous ethanol (AR grade), ethylene glycol (EG), and potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7). They are all purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China), aside from K2Cr2O7, which is purchased from Xilong Scientific (China). All reagents are of analytical reagent (AR) grade, and are used as received without further purification. In addition, ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ⋅cm−1, which is prepared using an a Direct-Q 5 UV System (Merck Millipore, Germany), is used throughout the current study.

Table 1 presents the proximate analysis and elementary constituents of the lignite sample. According to the Chinese classification of coal (GB/T 5751–2009), the coal sample is categorized into lignite. Furthermore, its total sulfur content is 3.06 %, making it a high-sulfur-containing lignite based on the China’s coal quality classification (GB/T 15224.2–2021). Therefore, its combustion for power generation is strictly prohibited in most Chinese cities due to large emissions of SO2. The synthesis of value-added GQDs provides an alternative to the clean use of this lignite.

Lignite-based GQDs (L-GQDs) were produced using a chemical oxidation method [30]. The main synthesis procedure is summarized as follows: 300 mg of lignite sample (with particle size less than 75 μm) was added to a mixed solvent consisting of 20 mL concentrated HNO3 and 60 mL concentrated H2SO4. The solvent was stirred for 0.5 h to ensure the uniform dispersion of the coal sample. The suspension was then sonicated at 30 °C for 2 h, followed by oxidization in an oil bath at 140 °C for 24 h. The resultant solution was poured into a beaker filled with 100 mL of ice to dilute and cool it to room temperature. Afterwards, its pH was adjusted to 7 using a 3 M NaOH solution. The neutral mixture was then filtered using a 0.45 μm polytetrafluoroethylene membrane to obtain a yellow filtrate, which was then dialyzed in a 1,000 Da dialysis bag for 4 days. The ultrapure water used for dialysis was changed every 12 h. Finally, the purified solution was concentrated in a rotary evaporator (N-1300, Eyela, Tokyo Rikakikai, Japan) to obtain solid L-GQDs samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of BiOBr and L-GQDs/BiOBr nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: BiOBr and Lignite-based GQDs/ Bismuth oxybromide (L-GQDs/BiOBr) composites were prepared via a semi-solvothermal method. Fig. 1 illustrates the preparation of L-GQDs/BiOBr composites. 4.32 mmol of Bi(NO3)3⋅5H2O and 4.32 mmol of KBr were dissolved in 36 mL of an EG solution. The mixture was continuously stirred for 30 min at room temperature to produce Solution A. Meanwhile, 0.05 – 0.25 g of prepared L-GQDs were dispersed in 40 mL of water to form Suspension B, which was then added into Solution A, resulting in a precursor suspension. After magnetically stirring for 60 min, the precursor suspension was loaded into a 100-mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave, and was heated at 140 °C for 12 h. After naturally cooling to room temperature, the obtained product was centrifuged, followed by washing with ultrapure water and anhydrous ethanol, three times each. The resultant samples were then dried at 60 °C for 12 h, referred to as 0.05L-GQDs/BOB, 0.15L-GQDs/BOB, and 0.25L-GQDs/BOB, corresponding to 0.05 g, 0.15 g, and 0.25 g of solid L-GQDs, respectively.
Pure BiOBr was prepared following the same procedure without adding L-GQDs, and is named as BOB for the ease of presentation [31].

X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker AXS D8 Advance, Germany) with Cu-κβ radiation and λ = 1.5418 Å was employed to detect the crystal phases of the samples. The stoichiometric composition and chemical states on the surface were analyzed via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo ECALAB 250xi system, USA) using an Al as the X-ray source. Microstructures and morphologies of photocatalysts were observed using a field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, Hitachi-SU8220, USA). Energy dispersive X-ray spectra (EDS) and elemental mappings were also conducted on the SEM instrument. Lattice spacings were determined by a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM, Tecnai G2-F20 FEI, USA). The light absorption characteristics was determined via UV–Vis diffuse reflection spectroscopy (UV–Vis DRS, Hitachi U-3900H, Japan) in the range of 190–800 nm, with BaSO4 as used the background. Specific surface area and pore size distribution of the samples were calculated using N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms collected on a high-performance micropore analyzer (Micromeritics ASAP 2460 3.01, USA). Photoluminescence (PL) spectrum was obtained through a transient fluorescence spectrometer (FS5, Edinburgh Instruments, UK) with an excitation wavelength of 320 nm. The active species generated during photocatalysis were determined using electron spin resonance (ESR, Bruker EMXplus, Germany). The anions deposited on the surface of the catalyst after photocatalysis were identify by ion chromatography (IC, Thermo Scientific Aquion, USA).

The photoelectrochemical measurements were performed on an electrochemical workstation (Model CHI660E, Shanghai Chenhua, China) with a standard three-electrode system. The reference electrode was a standard saturated calomel electrode (SCE), with the counter electrode being a Pt sheet, and the working electrode being a piece of indium-tin oxide (ITO) conductive glass coated with photocatalysts. A 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution was used as the electrolyte.

The working electrodes were prepared as follows: 7 mg of catalyst and 20 µl of Nafion solution (DuPont, China; 5 wt%) were added to 1 mL of ethanol and sonicated for 2 min to disperse the catalyst uniformly. Then 200 µl of the prepared mixture was coated on the ITO conductive glass with an area of 1 × 1 cm2, followed by drying at 60 °C for 3 h.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was recorded using the A.C. Impedance (IMP) method at a bias potential of 0.5 V, with an amplitude of 0.5 mV and a frequency range of 0.01 Hz to 10,000 Hz. Mott-Schottky (MS) curves were obtained by the impedance-potential (IMPE) method at a frequency of 1 kHz, a voltage of 5 mV and a scanning potential ranging from −1 V to 1 V. Both EIS and MS tests were carried out in dark conditions. However, the photocurrent responses were obtained by the Amperometric i-t Curve method using a 300 W Xe lamp (with 380 nm cut filter) light source, which was switched on and off every 20 s.

The photocatalytic activity of the prepared catalysts was evaluated by degrading NO at a flow rate of 200 mL/min and an inlet NO concentration of 33–35 ppm. The conversion rate of NO (η) and DeNOx index are used to assess the photocatalytic activity of the prepared catalyst and the environmental friendliness. The conversion rate represents the total degradation efficiency of NO within a given duration and is calculated using Eq. (1). In addition, Bloh et al. assumed that the toxic contribution of NO2 in NOx is three times that of NO, and consequently proposed the DeNOx index to quantify the environmental impact of photocatalytic NO degradation. The DeNOx index based on this assumption is expressed by Eq (2) [29].

The reaction activity of the prepared catalysts is tested as follows. First, a glass sheet (5 × 10 cm2) with 80 mg of catalyst is placed in the reactor before the introduction of a simulated gas stream. The system is left in the dark condition for 10 min to reach dynamic equilibrium between NO adsorption and desorption, observed by the continuous monitoring of NOx concentrations at the outlet using a FTIR analyzer (MKS MultiGas 2060, USA). The 300 W Xe lamp (λ ≥ 380 nm; the light intensity is 68.85 mW/cm2) is then switched on, and the photocatalytic reaction lasts for 1 h.

The stability of the catalyst is essential for industrial applications, and in this work, it is verified through cyclic tests. After each cycle, the used catalyst is regenerated via heating at 200 °C (with a ramp rate of 10 °C/min) for 2 h in a tube furnace. There are 5 cycles in total. In addition, scavenging experiments are performed to detect the production of superoxide radicals (O2 •-) and hydroxyl radicals (OH•) during the photocatalytic degradation of NO.

The optimal adsorption positions of L-GQDs onto BiOBr were obtained by DFT calculations. Subsequently, the charge transfer between L-GQDs and BiOBr, and the contribution of each element in the conduction bands and valence bands are calculated.

The generalized gradient approximation (GGA) with the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerh (PBE) functional was used to describe the exchange–correlation interactions. The convergence thresholds of the total energy, the force on each atom, and the maximum displacement were set to 1.0 × 10-5 eV·atom−1, 0.03 eV·Å−1, and 0.001 Å, respectively. Based on the XRD pattern and the HR-TEM characterization, the (102) crystal plane of BiOBr was selected as the adsorption plane for L-GQDs. To simplify the calculation, a structure model with 4 benzene rings containing 16 carbon atoms was chosen to represent L-GQDs.

It is noted that the L-GQDs model tends to adsorb onto the surface of exposed oxygen atoms. Therefore, four different positions, each exposed to oxygen atoms on the BiOBr (102) crystal surface were chosen as potential adsorption sites. The adsorption energy of each position is calculated using Eq (3):

Fig. 2 shows the crystalline structure of the prepared catalysts. The XRD patterns of all catalysts match the standard PDF card (JCPDS # 73–2061) of BiOBr [32]. The three strongest peaks are sharp and located at 25.40°, 31.94° and 32.46°, corresponding to the (011), (102) and (110) crystal planes of BiOBr, respectively. The characteristic peaks of L-GQDs are not observed in any XRD curve, which may be attributed to the small amount and low crystallinity of L-GQDs [16,33,34], as well as their uniform distribution on the BiOBr surface [31]. This indicates that the addition of L-GQDs does not alter the primary crystal structure of BiOBr.

Fig. 3 presents the XPS spectra for pure BOB and 0.15L-GQDs/BOB samples. All measurements are calibrated by C 1 s at 284.8 eV. Fig. 3a and d show the full-spectrum scans of BOB and 0.15L-GQDs/BOB, respectively. Both samples contain elements of C, O, Bi and Br, and the peak positions of these elements remain unchanged by the addition of L-GQDs.

Fig. 3b shows the high-resolution narrow-scan XPS spectrum of C 1 s obtained by multimodal fitting. The C 1 s of BOB can be divided into three peaks located at 284.8 eV, 286.1 eV, and 288.8 eV, corresponding to the C = C bond, C-OH bond, and O-C = O bond, respectively [35–37]. In contrast, for the 0.15L-GQDs/BOB sample, the C 1 s spectrum can be divided into four peaks located at 284.8 eV, 285.7 eV, 288.8 eV, and 286.7 eV, respectively. The additional peak located at 286.7 eV is assigned to the C-O bond, indicating the presence of GQDs [38].

In Fig. 3c, the O 1 s spectrum of BOB is deconvoluted into two peaks. The one located at 530.5 eV is designated to the Bi-O bond [37], whereas that at 532.0 eV is ascribed to the O–H bond [39]. In contrast, the O 1 s spectrum of 0.15L-GQDs/BOB is split into three peaks at 530.5 eV and 531.9 eV, and 532.9 eV, respectively. The first two align with those identified for pure BOB, while the additional peak at 532.9 eV corresponds to the C-O bond, again indicating the existence of GQDs [40]. The cross-comparison of the high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s and O 1 s between BOB and 0.15L-GQDs/BOB indicates that L-GQDs are successfully loaded onto the BOB surface in the 0.15L-GQDs/BOB composite.

Fig. 3e shows the fine spectra of Bi 4f for both BOB and 0.15L-GQDs/BOB materials. The two binding peaks at 159.7 eV and 165.0 eV are designated to the Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 spin states, respectively. The difference between the two peaks is 5.3 eV, indicating the presence of Bi3+ ions in the samples [41]. Fig. 3f presents the Br 3d fine spectra for BOB and 0.15L-GQDs/BOB. The peaks located at 68.8 eV and 69.8 eV correspond to Br 3d5/2 and Br 3d3/2, respectively [42]. In summary, the XPS results shows that L-GQDs are incorporated into the prepared 0.15L-GQDs/BOB composite without changing the valence states of original elements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface morphology and specific surface area
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 a and b show the SEM images of BOB and 0.15L-GQDs/BOB, respectively. BOB and 0.15L-GQDs/BOB are nanosheet structures. The average size of BOB nanosheets is approximately 200 nm, while the 0.15L-GQDs/BOB nanosheets range from 50 to 150 nm. The increased agglomeration in 0.15L-GQDs/BOB nanosheets is likely due to their smaller size. As particle size decreases, surface forces become more dominant, leading to agglomeration during sample preparation. The surface of 0.15L-GQDs/BOB is much rougher than pure BOB, which might be due to the loading of L-GQDs on the BOB surfaces. Fig. 4d-g show the EDS images of Fig. 4c for 0.15L-GQDs/BOB, where the elements Bi, C, Br and O can be observed on the catalyst surface, and are uniformly distributed. The EDS images illustrate that the L-GQDs are successfully loaded onto the BOB surface and uniformly distributed on the BOB surface, explaining the absence of corresponding diffraction peaks of L-GQDs in the XRD spectra of 0.15L-GQDs/BOB.

Fig. 5 shows the FE-TEM and HR-TEM images of BOB and 0.15L-GQDs/BOB. Fig. 5a shows the FE-TEM image of BOB, from which the size of BOB is found to be approximately 200 nm, which is consistent with the TEM characterization results. Fig. 5b shows the FE-SEM image of 0.15L-GQDs/BOB, where black dots on the surface of BOB correspond to L-GQDs (marked by red circles in the figure). Fig. 5c shows the HR-TEM image of BOB, in which bright interphase lattice stripes with a distance of 0.27 nm, corresponding to the (102) crystal plane of BOB can be clearly observed [42]. Fig. 5d shows the HR-TEM image of 0.15L-GQDs/BOB. In addition to the lattice distance corresponding to the (102) face of BOB, the bright interphase lattice stripe of L-GQDs with a distance of 0.21 nm can be observed [43]. Fig. 5e indicates that the size of the prepared L-GQDs (see the red circles containing lattice fringes) is less than 10 nm, falling within the size range of quantum dots. Meanwhile, L-GQDs with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm could be detected in Fig. 5f [43]. This further demonstrates the successful loading of L-GQDs onto the BOB catalyst surface.

The specific surface area and the pore structure of the prepared BOB and 0.15L-GQDs/BOB are analyzed using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method. The specific surface areas of BOB and 0.15L-GQDs/BOB are 10.10 and 31.81 m2/g, respectively, suggesting that the addition of L-GQDs increases surface area, thus in turn, providing more active sites for photocatalytic reactions. Fig. 6 a shows the isothermal adsorption–desorption curves of BOB and 0.15L-GQDs/BOB. The isotherms of BOB and 0.15L-GQDs/BOB belong to Type IV isotherm with an H3-type hysteresis loop, indicating the presence of mesopores in the samples [44,45]. The hysteresis loop of 0.15L-GQDs/BOB is significantly larger than that of BOB, reflecting that the specific surface area of 0.15L-GQDs/BOB is larger than that of BOB. This finding is consistent with the measurements of the BET specific surface area.

Fig. 6b shows the BJH pore size distribution curves of BOB and 0.15L-GQDs/BOB. The pore size distribution of the composite 0.15L-GQDs/BOB falls within the range of 10–25 nm. In contrast, the pore size of BOB monomer is smaller than that of 0.15L-GQDs/BOB. The pore volume of the 0.15L-GQDs/BOB composite is 0.18 cm3/g, which is 6 times higher than that of pristine BOB (0.03 cm3/g). The increased pore volume of the catalyst will provide more sites for NO adsorption, thereby promoting the photocatalytic degradation of NO.

Fig. 7 a presents the UV–Vis DRS spectra of BOB and different LG/BOB composites, which are measured to examine their light absorption ability and calculate the band gaps. All samples respond to visible lights, and the absorption of lights increases with increasing amounts of L-GQDs added. The addition of L-GQDs red shifted the absorption band of composite catalysts compared to pristine BOB. This may be due to the interaction between L-GQDs and BOB, which changes the electronic structure of catalyst surface. The band gaps of different catalysts were calculated using the K-M method using Eq (4) [46].

are the effective absorption coefficient, diffuse reflection coefficient, and scattering coefficient of the sample, respectively.

The band gaps are determined by plotting [F(R∞)hv] 1/2 vs. hv as shown in Fig. 7b. The values are 2.96 eV, 2.91 eV, 2.88 eV, and 2.74 eV for BOB, 0.05L-GQDs/BOB, 0.15L-GQDs/BOB, and 0.25L-GQDs/BOB, respectively. These results indicate that the addition of L-GQDs improves the catalyst’s ability to absorb visible lights and reduces the band gaps.

Fig. 8 a shows the MS curves for BOB and 0.15L-GQDs/BOB samples. The slopes of the curves for both BOB and 0.15L-GQDs/BOB are positive, indicating that they are both n-type semiconductors [47]. This suggests that the addition of L-GQDs does not affect the semiconductor type of catalyst. According to Fig. 8a, the flat-band potentials, Efb, of BOB and 0.15L-GQDs/BOB samples are −0.71 V and −0.68 V vs. SCE, respectively. These values are then further adjusted to −0.47 V and −0.44 V vs. NHE using the equation, E(NHE) = E(SCE) + 0.24 V [48]. Because the potential of the conduction band (CB) edge of the n-type semiconductor is 0.1 V more negative than that of Efb [49], the CB edges potentials of BOB and 0.15L-GQDs/BOB are calculated to be −0.57 V and −0.54 V vs. NHE. Based on the formula, EVB = Eg + ECB , the valence band (VB) potentials of BOB and 0.15L-GQDs/BOB are computed as 2.39 V and 2.34 V vs. NHE, respectively.

Fig. 8b and c display the XPS VB spectra of samples, from which the VB energies of BOB and 0.15L-GQDs/BOB are determined to be 2.26 eV and 2.24 eV, respectively. The VB energies at the vacuum energy level are then adjusted to 6.81 eV and 6.79 eV after incorporating the work function of the XPS instrument, being 4.55 eV. Furthermore, since the vacuum energy level of 4.44 eV corresponds to 0 V vs. NHE [50,51], the valence band potentials of BOB and 0.15L-GQDs/BOB can be calculated to be 2.37 V and 2.35 V vs. NHE, respectively. These values closely consistent with those calculated from the MS plotting, showing the band structure of photocatalysts are determined reliably.

Fig. 8d shows the PL spectra of pure BOB and 0.15L-GQDs/BOB. When the excitation wavelength is 320 nm, both PL spectra have similar shapes and their main peaks are located at 540 nm. The PL spectra reflects the recombination of photogenerated carriers; a higher peak intensity implies a faster recombination of photogenerated carriers. Therefore, photocatalysts with lower PL intensities typically exhibit higher photocatalytic activity [52,53]. The intensity of the primary peak of 0.15L-GQDs/BOB is lower than that of BOB, indicating that the addition of L-GQDs inhibits the electron-hole recombination, and allows the carriers to be transferred to catalyst surface for photocatalytic reactions.

Fig. 8e shows the EIS plots of BOB and 0.15L-GQDs/BOB. EIS is an effective electrochemical method for assessing the efficiency of electron transfer within a catalyst. A lower resistance to photogenerated electron transfer inside the catalyst results in a smaller diameter of the arc of the Nyquist curve in the EIS plot [54,55]. The diameter of 0.15L-GQDs/BOB is significantly smaller than that of BOB, indicating that the addition of L-GQDs reduces the resistance of electron transfer and promotes the separation of photogenerated carriers, thus enhancing photocatalytic activity.

Transient photocurrent measurement is an effective method for comprehensively investigating the light responses and the separation of photogenerated carriers in photocatalysts. As shown in Fig. 8f, BOB and 0.15L-GQDs/BOB exhibit fast and stable photocurrent responses upon illumination, despite different intensity [56,57]. The photocurrent response of 0.15L-GQDs/BOB is much greater than that of BOB, which indicates that 0.15L-GQDs/BOB is superior in terms of separation of carriers. This finding echoes the EIS results.

Fig. S1a-c present the time-series concentrations of NO, NO2 and gaseous HNO2 over a one-hour period. These data are subsequently employed to compute the η (see Fig. 9 a) and DeNOx index (see Fig. 9b) according to Eqs. (1) and (2). Fig. 9a shows the photocatalytic degradation of NO using different catalysts under visible-light irradiation. The NO degradation efficiency over 1 h increases rapidly from 48.44 % to 80.17 % with the increase in the amount of L-GQDs, followed by a slight decrease to 77.47 %. The NO photodegradation efficiency of L-GQDs/BOB composites is 1.60––1.66 times higher than that of BOB samples. This is attributed to the addition of L-GQDs, which increases the specific surface area of the catalyst, improves the absorption of visible lights, and promotes the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes. The degradation efficiency of 0.05L-GQDs /BOB is lower than that of 0.15L-GQDs/BOB, possibly due to insufficient deposition of L-GQDs on the surface of BOB. Conversely, the catalytic activity of 0.25L-GQDs/BOB is decreased compared to 0.15L-GQDs/BOB. This is because excessive amounts of L-GQDs occupy the reactive sites of NO. Therefore, an optimum dosage of L-GQDs exists, with 0.15L-GQDs/BOB being preferable for NO conversion efficiency in this study.

Fig. 9b shows the DeNOx indices of different photocatalysts. A more positive DeNOx index indicates that the prepared catalysts are more environmentally friendly during the photocatalytic degradation of NO. All L-GQDs/BOB composites outperformed pure BOB in terms of DeNOx index, and the highest DeNOx index of 0.34 is achieved for 0.25L-GQDs sample, which is much higher than that of −0.73 for pristine BOB. The DeNOx indices of 0.05L-GQDs/BOB and 0.15L-GQDs/BOB are −0.09 and 0.18, respectively, which are also greater than that of BOB. These findings indicate that the addition of L-GQDs can improve the environmental friendliness of NO photocatalysis using BOB as the catalyst.

Fig. 9c shows the instantaneous degradation efficiencies of BOB and L-GQDs/BOB composites during the photocatalytic degradation of NO. The photocatalytic degradation efficiency of BOB decreases rapidly from 86.42 % to 30.50 % within 1 h. Conversely, the NO degradation efficiency of 0.15L-GQDs/BOB decreases slowly from 95.44 % to 78.64 %. Such findings suggest that the presence of L-GQDs not only enhances NO degradation efficiency, but also inhibits the deactivation of photocatalysts.

Fig. 9d shows the time-series concentrations of NO, NO2, and HNO2 at the outlet of the 0.15L-GQDs/BOB catalyst, where
C
N
out
represents the sum of NO, NO2, and HNO2 concentrations. During the photocatalytic degradation of NO,
C
N
out
is always lower than the inlet concentration of NO. This suggests that NO is not fully converted to NO2 and HNO2 in the gas phase. Instead, it is oxidized to nitrogen-containing substances deposited on the photocatalyst surface. The conversion of nitrogen (N) is important for understanding the mechanism behind NO photocatalysis. Therefore, the used photocatalysts were washed by ultrapure water. The IC measurements of the wash solution evidenced that
N O
3
-
and
N O
2
-
formed and were deposited on the catalyst surface during the photocatalytic degradation of NO. Therefore, it is inferred that during NO photocatalysis using 0.15L-GQDs/BOB composite, NO is not only converted to gaseous products, including NO2 and HNO2, but also partially converted into
N O
3

and
N O

ions on the catalyst surface.

Fig. 10 shows the NO photodegradation efficiency of five consecutive cycles using the 0.15L-GQDs/BOB composite. The efficiency of NO photodegradation remained nearly constant after 3 cycles, with a slight decrease observed when the cyclic number further increases. Even at the fifth cycle, it maintains 94.40 % of the reactive activity exhibited by the fresh catalyst. This indicates that the prepared catalysts possess good chemical stability during NO photodegradation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism behind photocatalytic degradation of NO
ce:section.ce:para: The main active species during the NO photocatalysis of 0.15L-GQDs/BOB composite is determined by scavenger tests. Fig. 11 a shows that the degradation efficiency of NO within 1 h decreases from 80.17 % to 53.39 % when H2O is removed from the inlet gas mixture. When O2 is removed from the gas mixture, the degradation efficiency of NO within 1 h drops dramatically to 24.83 %. Such results indicate that O2 •- is the primary active substance in the photocatalytic degradation of NO, followed by OH•.

To further verify the generation of O2 •- and OH• during the photocatalytic degradation of NO, ESR tests were performed under both dark and visible-light irradiation conditions [58,59]. The results are presented in Fig. 11c and d. Both O2 •- and OH• spectra have none response in the dark, and exhibit four signal peaks under visible-light irradiation. This implies that light is necessary to produce O2 •- and OH•. In addition, the signal peak intensity of O2 •- is stronger than that of OH•, indicating that O2 •- plays a more important role than OH• in the photocatalytic process. This is consistent with the experimental results of scavenger tests.

DFT calculations are performed to elucidate the adsorption sites of L-GQDs and the charge transfer between L-GQDs and BOB. Fig. 12 presents the four potential sites for the adsorption of L-GQDs. After optimizing the structure of L-GQDs/BOB, the adsorption energy at each position and the distance between C elements of the L-GQDs and the O elements on the BOB are calculated. The corresponding results are summarized in Table 2 . The adsorption energy at Position 2 is found to be smaller (-1.69 eV) compared to its counterparts at Positions 1, 3 and 4. Furthermore, the distance between C and O elements at Position 2 is 3.025 Å, which is shorter than those at Positions 1, 3 and 4. Such information indicates that the adsorption between L-GQDs and BOB is the strongest at Position 2. Therefore, Position 2 is selected for subsequent calculations.

Fig. 13 a and b are the top and main views of the charge density difference, respectively, illustrating the charge transfer between L-GQDs and BOB. The blue and yellow regions represent the decrease of electron density and the accumulation of electrons, respectively. The color difference indicates that the electrons accumulate around the L-GQDs. Specifically, the Bader charge calculation reveal a transfer of 0.2 electrons from BOB to the surface of L-GQDs. This electron transfer establishes a built-in electric field directing from BOB to L-GQDs. When the L-GQDs/BOB composite is irradiated by visible lights, the photoexcited electrons on the surface of the L-GQDs migrate to BOB due to the built-in electric field. This finding is consistent with the projected density of states (PDOS) of the L-GQDs/BOB in Fig. 14 c.

Fig. 14a and b present the PDOS of pure BOB. The valence band maximum (VBM) consists of Br 3p, O 2p and Bi 6 s orbits, whereas the conduction band minimum (CBM) consists of Bi 6p and O 2p orbits. Fig. 14c and d show that after adding L-GQDs, the orbit components of VBM and CBM change. Specifically, the VBM of L-GQDs/BOB composite is constituted by partial C 2p orbit, and its CBM is composed by Bi 6p, O 2p and C 2p empty orbits. The introduction of C 2p orbits to the CBM shifts the CBM to the left; therefore, the band gap of the composite is narrowed. This responds to the XPS-VB measurements and UV–Vis DRS spectra.

Fig. 15 a and b show the band structures of BOB and L-GQDs/BOB composite determined by DFT calculations. The band gaps of pure BOB and L-GQDs/BOB are 2.12 eV and 1.72 eV, respectively. The DFT theoretical calculations reveal that the addition of L-GQDs reduces the band gap of the BOB photocatalyst, which is consistent with the findings from UV–Vis DRS measurements. It is worth noting that the underestimation in the calculated band gaps stems from the inherent limitation of DFT calculations using the GGA functional [60].

The CBM and VBM of BOB and L-GQDs/BOB were experimentally measured using MS and UV–Vis measurements. Utilizing the knowledge of the band structure of L-GQDs, the heterojunction formed between L-GQDs and BOB can be determined. The L-GQDs materials exhibit a non-zero band gap, and their CB potential is −0.58 V [28], which is more negative than that of BOB (-0.57 V). The electrons in the conduction band of L-GQDs tend to transfer to the conduction band of BOB due to potential difference, diminishing the combination of photogenerated electrons and holes. The valence band of L-GQDs/BOB is located on the L-GQDs according to DFT calculations. Furthermore, the valence band potential of L-GQDs/BOB is ascertained to be 2.35 V through both MS and XPS-VB tests, while the valence band potential of BOB is found to be 2.39 V, exceeding the 2.35 V potential of L-GQDs/BOB. Therefore, the holes in the valence band of BOB can be transported to the valence band of L-GQDs during photoexcitation. As a result, L-GQDs and BOB form type II heterojunctions in L-GQDs/BOB composites, which greatly facilitates the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes, thus improving the photocatalytic performance of the composite catalysts.

According to the redox potential theory, the reactions involved in the photocatalytic degradation of NO can be determined. MS tests obtain composite 0.15L-GQDs/BOB with a conduction band of −0.54 V (vs. NHE), which is more negative than the potential of O2/ O2 •- (-0.33 V vs. NHE) [61]. This suggests that the photogenerated electrons generated during the photocatalytic process can reduce the O2 molecules adsorbed on the catalyst to O2 •- according to Eq. (5). NO then reacts with O2 •- to produce the end products including NO2, NO2 − and NO3 − ions (see Eqs. (8–10)) [62–64].

In addition, the valence band potential of 0.15L-GQDs/BOB is calculated to be 2.35 V (vs. NHE) by XPS-VB test, which indicates that photogenerated holes can oxidize OH– to OH• (OH–/ OH•, 1.99 V vs. NHE) [65] as shown in Eq.(6). The generated OH• can react with NO to form NO2 [66], which can continue to react with OH• to form HNO2, NO2 − and NO3 − (see Eqs. (11–14)) [67,68]. Moreover, the generated NO2 can react with O2 •- to form NO3 − (Eq. (15) [69].

Based on DFT theoretical calculations, scavenger experiments, UV-DRS measurements and MS tests, the mechanism of NO photodegradation by L-GQDs/BOB can be summarized using Eqs. (5–16). Fig. 16 illustrates the proposed mechanism.

In the current work, we synthesize value-added L-GQDs from low-rank lignite, and present a novel visible-light-responsive L-GQDs/BOB composite prepared via a semi-solvothermal method. Through rigorous characterizations including XPS, EDS, SEM, and HR-TEM, we successfully demonstrate the effective loading of L-GQDs onto the BiOBr surface. The 0.15L-GQDs/BOB composite achieves remarkable performance in NO photodegradation. Compared to pure BiOBr, it boosts NO removal efficiency from 48.44 % to 80.17 % and improves the DeNOx index from −0.73 to 0.18, underscoring its potential as an efficient photocatalyst for NO abatement. In addition, the cyclic experiments attest to the chemical stability of the prepared catalysts, showing great potential for practical applications.

Our investigation reveals that O2 •- serves as the primary active species in photocatalytic degradation of NO, while OH• plays a secondary role. Furthermore, the formation of a type II heterojunction between L-GQDs and BOBs within the composite significantly enhances the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes, thereby elevating overall photocatalytic activity.
In conclusion, this work not only contributes to the photodegradation of NO pollutants, but also offers an environmentally conscious alternative to lignite utilization.
